channel.title: Teacher Plus
channel.description: The magazine for the contemporary teacher

item.title: Curiosity, interest, respect
item.dc:creator: Usha Raman
item.content:encoded:
Three words that should be the starting point for all learning, but, according to evolutionary biologist Prof Raghavendra Gagadkar, they are attitudes fundamental to studying the natural world. Recently, I had the pleasure of listening to Prof Gagadkar speak to a multi-generational audience about his work on insect societies and what we can learn from them. Among the listeners were several young people, whose keen interest was evident in the numerous questions they asked, often interrupting the speaker (who had already made it clear that he wanted interaction) and eliciting his delighted responses. Weaving through the fascinating worlds of ants, honeybees, and wasps, the professor drew lessons on collective intelligence, resource optimization, and decision-making that have found their way into human infrastructures.

Among the many interesting points that he made was one that we might do well to pay particular attention to: science is too important to be left to scientists. What this suggests is that we need to think of science less as a subject that one specializes in, and more as a system of knowledge building and a way of understanding the world that everyone can engage with. Of course, this does not diminish the importance of scientific research as a specialized activity that requires focused training and many years of dedicated study – we do need people who do that. But it points to a need for all of us to watch, think, and make critical judgments that make use of science, but at times even question it using the same tools of observation and logic that scientists use. When one young person asked the professor whether and how insects respond to human intervention, his answer was simple: “The next time you see a line of ants, why don’t you try to break the line, introduce an obstacle, and see what happens?” Many of us may have done this, even unthinkingly, in the course of our daily lives, but rarely do we dwell on the action to see what happens next – and that’s what takes us to the learning.

But again, we all learn in such different ways (as our cover story this month explains), and choose to internalize the lessons that are of most use to us. People who work on the land or in the forest have evolved practices that are based on generations of observing weather patterns, soil quality, and plant behaviour. They may codify their knowledge differently, but it is no less systematic. These knowledges, much like the collective intelligence of social insects, is transmitted across generations in both discernible and intangible ways.

So how do we build curiosity, interest, and respect in our students, such that they are able to approach the natural world in productive ways? I suppose that’s the billion rupee question for all educators. Some of the columns (such as Ecowatch) in Teacher Plus have been attempting to do this in simple yet granular ways. If you have evolved your own strategies for sustaining respectful curiosity, do let us know.
item.excerpt:encoded: Three words that should be the starting point for all learning, but, according to evolutionary biologist Prof Raghavendra Gagadkar, they are attitudes fundamental to studying the natural world.

item.title: Be the change you want to see in the world
item.dc:creator: shalini
item.content:encoded:
Anwar J Rayma

India is a secular country. However, Indian secularism differs from that in the West. Here, the meaning of secularism is accepted and adopted as "Sarvadharma Samabhav" (equal respect for all religions), rather than the rejection of religion altogether. In India, no bias is shown toward any single religion, and this is exactly what has been taught in school textbooks for years.

I have spent over twenty years as a teacher, and my father was also a teacher. I have always held a deep sense of respect for my teachers, and my father shared a very close bond with his friends in the teaching profession. One thing I have always believed is that when a teacher enters their classroom, they are not a representative of any single religion; they are a Guru, whose primary duty is to provide the correct education to children.

They must not only teach their subject but also impart moral values. In my opinion, if a teacher enters the class with a bias toward any specific religion, they are betraying their profession. Just as a doctor cannot approach patients of a specific religion with prejudice in their heart, the role of a teacher is similar.

Today, I wonder if teachers are not performing their duties with integrity, or they are unable to do so. At the same time, it is important to note that people with prejudice have polluted the education system.

Take the incident at a school in Mundra, Kutch (Gujarat), or the case in a school in Anjar. In these instances, children put on a cultural program simply to demonstrate how the festival of Eid is celebrated. Yet, parents gathered at the schools to protest against the celebration.

I wonder why these parents harbour such fears because no religious text teaches extremism or hatred toward another religion.

Personally, I have watched the Ramayana and Mahabharata since childhood and read a lot about them. In both of these great epics, there is not a single character or verse that teaches such petty thinking.

If any parents truly want to teach their children about their religion or about the values of humanity, they can do so through the atmosphere of their home and their own conduct. They need not go to schools and create theatrical scenes. Participating in the celebration of any religion’s festival is necessary to strengthen children’s secular views.

In a society as religiously diverse as India, having harmony and respect for one another's religion is absolutely essential. Schools only want to show children how different cultures and religions celebrate festivals, so that children can learn about the diversity in our country and the world at large.

Anwar J Rayma, is a seasoned educator with 20 plus years of experience. The author's practice is fundamentally grounded in child and educational psychology. A dedicated practitioner of activity-based learning, methodology consistently centered on Bloom's Taxonomy to design engaging, high-order thinking activities for students. He can be reached at anwarrayma209@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Anwar J Rayma
Sarvadharma Samabhav is the Indian philosophy of secularism. It emphasizes the role of educators in upholding neutrality and moral integrity at school and in the classroom. However, rising parental resistance to interfaith cultural exposure undermines the school’s efforts towards social cohesion. According to the author, exposing children to different festivals and cultures is vital for nurturing respect, empathy, and pluralism in a diverse society like India.

item.title: Curiosity: the seed of scientific thinking in children
item.dc:creator: shalini
item.content:encoded:
Sunita Ahuja

When children begin to ask “why?”, it marks the beginning of deeper thinking and exploration. This natural curiosity encourages them to observe, question, and wonder about the world     . Such inquiry lays the foundation for      scientific temper, where children seek evidence, test ideas, and learn through real experiences rather than memorization. For example, when a child asks, “Why do balloons float?” or “Why does the moon change shape?”, they are not just seeking answers—they are developing an early habit of inquiry and reasoning.

Hands-on activities, playful experiments, and open-ended discussions allow children to think independently and creatively. Simple moments such as watching rain evaporate from the balcony, observing ants carry food, or testing which objects a magnet sticks to, can help children build understanding through exploration. Activities like mixing colours to create      new shades, making clay models, or creating ramps for toy cars teach physics, creativity, and experimentation without formal instruction.

When adults respond with patience, encouragement, and curiosity in these pursuits     , children feel confident to express their ideas and try new approaches. Instead of giving immediate answers, asking questions like, “What do you think will happen if we change this?” or “Can we try another way?” helps them reflect, test their ideas, and evaluate outcomes.

Gradually, children begin applying concepts into daily life—like adjusting the amount of water in dough while helping in the kitchen, comparing shadows during different times of the day, or sorting dry leaves and flowers during nature walks. These experiences help them understand patterns, develop logical reasoning, and build problem-solving habits.

Over time, curiosity becomes more than just a question—it becomes a mindset. It nurtures resilience, confidence, innovation, and the ability to connect theory with real-world application. By fostering this culture of inquiry, we are shaping children to become thoughtful, analytical, and creative thinkers capable of meaningful decision-making and scientific understanding in everyday life.

Sunita Ahuja has over 17 years of experience in early childhood education. She has been passionately working as a pre-primary teacher, nurturing young minds and fostering a love for learning. For the past 10 years, she has been associated with Reliance Foundation School, Mouda, where she continues to contribute towards creating a positive and engaging learning environment for children. She can be reached at sunitaahuja700@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Sunita Ahuja
Hands-on experiences, open-ended dialogue, and adult guidance are vital ingredients for nurturing children’s independent thinking and problem-solving skills. Moreover, cultivating curiosity early on ensures that it becomes a lifelong mindset that then brings out children’s creativity, resilience, and real-world understanding.

item.title: Ancient Indian wisdom and modern pedagogy: a conceptual exploration for modern classrooms
item.dc:creator: shalini
item.content:encoded:
Pramila Kudva

Education in ancient India was far more than the transmission of academic content. It was an integrated process aimed at the holistic development of the learner. The Gurukul tradition placed equal emphasis on the physical, intellectual, emotional, moral, and spiritual domains. Knowledge was not compartmentalized but was interwoven with life and values. The ‘guru shishya Parampara’ had its roots in the rich culture of the country.

In the National Education Policy (NEP) 2020, there is a renewed emphasis on holistic, inclusive, and experiential learning. In this context, revisiting India’s own intellectual traditions becomes both a cultural responsibility and a pedagogical opportunity. Ancient frameworks such as the Pramanas (epistemological tools), Panchakosha (five sheaths of development), and Panchapadi (five-step instructional model) hold enduring relevance in shaping a curriculum that develops the head, heart, and hand in unison. This conceptual inquiry does not propose a return to the past. It makes an effort to weave time-tested Indian educational principles into the evolving fabric of 21st-century schooling and upgrade it.

Core concepts from ancient Indian educational thought

Panchakosha and Panchapadi deal with a sophisticated understanding of human cognition and development but also emphasize the moral and spiritual dimensions of education which are equally important.

Pramanas: ways of knowing and learning

In ancient Indian epistemology, Pramanas are recognized as valid means of acquiring knowledge. Among the six traditionally accepted Pramanas, the following stand out for their relevance to educational contexts:

Pratyaksha (Perception): Direct sensory experience, foundational to experiential learning.

Anumana (Inference): Logical deduction based on observation; parallels Piaget’s schema theory.

Upamana (Analogy): Learning through comparison and similarity; supports conceptual understanding.

Arthapatti (Postulation): Reasoning based on circumstantial evidence; useful in inquiry-based learning.

Anupalabdhi (Non-perception): Understanding through absence; encourages subtle observational skills.

Shabda (Testimony): Knowledge from trusted authority (e.g., guru, teacher, expert); aligns with Vygotsky’s MKO (More Knowledgeable Other).

These Pramanas can be mapped to elements of modern learning theory, particularly Kolb’s experiential learning cycle. They encourage learners to actively construct knowledge, reflect critically, and validate understanding from multiple sources.

Comparing Pramanas with Kolb's learning cycle:

Pratyaksha – direct perception, sensorial experiences which can be the ‘Concrete experience’.

Anumana – Inferences drawn out of observation can be akin to ‘Reflective observation’.

Upamana – knowing through analogy and comparison is comparable with ‘Active experimentation’.

Arthapatti – knowing through circumstantial implication can be related to ‘Abstract conceptualization’.

Anupalabdi – Perception of non-existence is also considered a valid form of knowledge. (“Indian School of thought in the NCF - Part 1 - LinkedIn”) – “The dogs did not bark at night” is an example given in the NCF 2023. We infer the reason for the dog's silence. This does not find a parallel in Kolb's cycle.

Sabda – the testimony of an expert is sometimes considered as true Knowledge – voice of the guru. The teacher or the Guru is the facilitator of the knowledge. Although there is no mention of this stage in Kolb's learning cycle, it is assumed that the teacher must play a role of facilitator and therefore, there is a match.

All this is captured in the following figure.

Panchakosha: the five sheaths of holistic human development

Panchakosha, from the Taittiriya Upanishad, presents a layered model of human existence, each representing a dimension of development. This framework corresponds well with the goals of holistic education as envisioned by NEP 2020 and the ECCE framework     .

Table 1 Panchakosha

The Panchakosha framework reminds educators that learners’ growth cannot be confined to cognitive milestones alone. Integrating this understanding can enrich curriculum design during school years. Here are a few other theoretical frameworks that can be linked to the Panchakosha model, offering a more holistic understanding of human nature.

Piaget and Panchakoshas

Panchakoshas are compared to Piaget’s stages of development.

Annamay Kosha – pre-operational stage of Piaget, relevant to the Foundation level.

Pranamaya – concrete operations of Piaget, relevant to the Preparatory stage.

Manomaya – formal operations of Piaget, relevant to Middle school.

Vijyanamaya & Anandmaya – formal operations of Piaget, continued relevance to the secondary and higher secondary levels.

Maslow's Hierarchy of Needs: a motivational parallel

Abraham Maslow's renowned model about the hierarchy of needs presents a compelling Western psychological parallel to the Panchakosha model. Both frameworks are hierarchical and progressive, suggesting that the fulfillment of lower-level needs or the purification of outer sheaths is foundational for the emergence of higher-level needs and the experience of the inner sheaths.

Annamaya Kosha (Food Sheath) and Pranamaya Kosha (Energy Sheath) align with Maslow's Physiological and Safety Needs. Just as the physical body requires sustenance and security, these foundational sheaths emphasize the importance of a healthy, stable physical existence and a balanced flow of life-force energy.

Manomaya Kosha (Mental Sheath) resonates with Maslow's Love and Belonging Needs. This sheath, which governs our thoughts, emotions, and desires, is deeply intertwined with our social connections and the need for acceptance and affection. Imbue sensitivity through socio-emotional learning quality circles or discussion as per the level of the students.

Vijnanamaya Kosha (Wisdom or Intellectual Sheath) corresponds to Maslow's Esteem Needs. This is the sheath of intellect, discernment, and self-awareness. Fulfilling esteem needs, such as achieving competence and gaining recognition, strengthens this kosha, leading to a more refined understanding of oneself and the world.

Anandamaya Kosha (Bliss Sheath) finds its parallel in Maslow's concept of Self-Actualization and Self-Transcendence. The aim of both frameworks is to reach a state of profound fulfillment, inner peace, and a connection to something larger than oneself. The experience of ananda (bliss) is the hallmark of a self-actualized individual who has transcended  ego-driven pursuits.

Panchapadi: the five step instructional process

The Panchapadi model, attributed to thinkers like Gijubhai Badheka and Swami Dayanand Saraswati, outlines a sequenced, student-centric learning approach that closely mirrors modern constructivist and inquiry-based methods:

Aditi (Introduction): Connect new learning with prior knowledge—activates neural pathways.

Bodh (Understanding): Deep engagement through dialogue, exploration, and meaning-making.

Abhyas (Practice): Reinforcement through varied activities—individual and collaborative.

Prayog (Application): Real-life application to internalize learning. Project-based learning is a good place to start.

Prasar (Expansion): Sharing with peers, teaching others, or expressing understanding. Peer learning and creativity could be implemented.

Panchapadi resonates with today’s emphasis on spiral curriculum, experiential learning, and learner autonomy. Panchapadi is like the learning process that is being practised to date. A parallel is drawn in the figure 3. 

Neuro-science tells us today that new knowledge should be linked to the old so that it can stay in the long-term memory. Rote learning had no place in the learning process. Practice leads to competence. Learning should not be such that it is imparted but not retrieved when required. It should be related to life. This is what makes learning long-lasting. Teaching should be based on ability. Differentiation is the term used by NEP. The steps of learning as expressed in Panchapadi are totally in sync with the modern pedagogy.

Alignment with SDG 4 (Quality Education)

Learning is the cornerstone of growth; it empowers us to shape our futures with knowledge and confidence.

Mahabharata tells us that Arjuna would practice archery in the dark by listening to the sounds - Shabdavedi – learning beyond what is taught. In today’s parlance, it would be competency-based learning. Each of the Pandavas was skilled in a different weapon based on their ability.      Modern Pedagogy would refer to it as differentiation! The Guru-Shishya Parampara or the gurukul education system instilled in the shishyas the capacity to think, reason, dialogue, develop sensitivity, humility and above all, the art of military warfare as per the need. The shishya even collected firewood for the ashram along with the others – a value of dignity of labour. Everyone was treated equitably. At the end of the training, the shishya would emerge as a holistic personality.

At Draupadi’s swayamvar, the one seeking the hand of Draupadi had to see the shadow of the fish in the oil below and shoot the eye of the fish which would be rotating above. The shooter had to keep in perspective the following physics principles. Light bends while travelling from one medium to another, which is refraction. The speed of the arrow and the speed of the rotation and the time taken by the rotating object to reach his point of focus. Arjuna could do it and win the hand of Draupadi. This was the competency that gurukul provided.

NEP and holistic education Provisions

Ancient Indian seers left no stone untouched to get to the top of the rung no matter what subject it was. Banabhatta in his ‘Kadambari’ describes good Education as something that should have 64 kalas or arts. These comprised singing or dancing, chemistry, mathematics, carpentry, tailoring, medicine, engineering, discussion, debating and communication. This choice of subjects is exactly what is needed for 21st century skills. A holistic multidisciplinary approach would aim to develop intellectual, aesthetic, social, physical, emotional, and moral in an integrated manner.

All HEIs (higher education institutions) will include credit-based courses and projects. They will also offer value-based education and environmental education. The undergrad course will have multiple exit options. The students' credits will be digitally stored. Multidisciplinary Education and Research Universities [MERU] will be set up with the sole purpose of attaining high-quality education. Such an education will help develop individuals with critical 21st century capacities across fields like      arts, humanities, languages, sciences, social sciences, and so on. Themes such as communication, discussion, and debate, as well as rigorous specialization in a chosen field or fields, are suggested to be included in the curriculum. All students from HEI will be provided with internship opportunities.      Provisions are made for research internships so that students may work on the practical side of their learning. Career counselling in schools towards identifying student interests and talents, promoting research in universities have found their way into the curriculum. 

Need for action

Quality education includes teaching holistically – not to teach in silos but to use interdisciplinary techniques, teach contextually, and use experiential learning     .      Life skills are to be introduced and technology wherever needed.

In short, the techniques could be represented as given in figure 4.

Implications and recommendations

Teacher training is critical in ensuring that NEP is implemented. These concepts need to make their way into the B.Ed syllabus. Practising teachers need to be made aware of these concepts by awareness building exercises – lectures, quizzes and so on. These traditional Indian concepts are culled out from the Vedas and Upanishads which are pre-1000 BCE. In comparison, Kolb’s cycle is only about 40 years old. If we were to create a time line, Piaget 1936, Maslow 1943, Erickson 1950 are young theories. Every student should be aware of the cultural heritage of the civilization and take pride in it.

REFERENCES

  1. https://soeonline.american.edu/blog/what-is-holistic-education
  2. https://www.simplypsychology.org/piaget.html
  3. https://www.verywellmind.com/erik-eriksons-stages-of-psychosocial-development-2795740
  4. https://www.simplypsychology.org/maslow.html
  5. https://www.simplypsychology.org/carl-rogers.html
  6. https://www.verywellmind.com/carl-jung-biography-archetypes-theories-beliefs-7556254
  7. E210IN826G0#id=1&vid=51ffe9ce08252a228a43e518116985c1&action=click
  8. https://teachers.institute/education-nature-purposes/gijubhai-badheka-child-centered-education/
  9. Kudva, Pramila & Mungekar Pratik Rajan [2023], Ancient Indian thoughts and Modern Pedagogy, Silent Revolution, 121 – 134.
  10. Kudva, Pramila & Mungekar Pratik Rajan [2023], Embracing a Holistic Learning in Education, Silent Revolution, 87 – 119.
  11. NEP 2020, Ministry of Human Resource, Govt of India, 2022.

Dr. (Mrs.) Pramila Narendra Kudva is an educational innovator, former ICSE principal, teacher educator, author, and a speaker. With decades of experience in school leadership and teacher training, she writes on holistic education, sustainable development, and the integration of Indian wisdom with contemporary pedagogy. She can be reached at pramilakudva2016@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Pramila Kudva
Ancient Indian educational frameworks such as Pramanas, Panchakosha, and Panchapadi offer sophisticated models of cognition, development, and instruction that align closely with modern pedagogical theories. By drawing parallels with thinkers like Piaget, Maslow, and Kolb, the article makes the case for an integrated approach that blends indigenous wisdom with contemporary educational goals under NEP 2020. Doing so will aid in the creation of a holistic, interdisciplinary, and culturally rooted education system that develops students’ intellectual, emotional, and ethical capacities.

item.title: Retiring the black star: a new era of compassion in classrooms
item.dc:creator: shalini
item.content:encoded:
Milonee Doshi

Imagine a classroom where every child feels safe to make mistakes, where feedback is a bridge, not a barrier. In such a space, the black star has no place. It's time we rethink how we respond to behaviour, not with shame, but with empathy. For example, teachers often give a black star to students who don't follow instructions, with the idea of helping them become more obedient.

When I was new to my teaching role, I, too, gave the black star to one of the students in my class. He immediately erased it. I gently asked him, “Why did you erase it?” He whispered, “I like black as it represents my favourite superhero, BatMan, and he always does good work, and so do I. Why is the black star the only one used, and not any other colour?”

This moment stayed with me, not because of the star, but because of a peek into how children come to associate colours. I realized that a black star can hurt the child's emotions, and that certainly is not the right way to teach discipline to students.

I suppose a black star has been used to discipline unruly behaviour in the classroom for a long time. Further, the colour has also come to be associated with punishment and negativity in culture (black sheep, dark times). These references portray the colour as undesirable. However, the colour black also represents strength, confidence, clarity and elegance.

This incident was a ray of hope for me, and it prompted me to start transitioning from a traditional to an innovative and open-ended approach. I started giving stickers of sad emojis (of any colour) to the students for not listening to my instructions or behaving badly in the class, as well as reflection cards, which help them to pause and think. This method helps them to connect more with their mistakes, as well as opens a ground for connection

between the educators and the students.

These small changes can have a significant impact, and it's essential to reevaluate such outdated practices. For decades, classrooms have used stars — gold for praise, black for punishment. But in a world that's evolving toward emotional intelligence and mental well-being, is it time we retire the black star?

Milonee Doshi is an enthusiastic educator who is passionate about teaching English as a language rather than just a subject. She can be reached at doshimilonee30@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Milonee Doshi
Classrooms often use gold stars to reward students and black stars as punitive measures. Instead of disciplining students as intended, such practices can shame and emotionally harm students. Additionally, children interpret feedback differently than adults, which means we need more empathetic approaches for classroom management.

item.title: Climate: an element of negotiation and participation
item.dc:creator: shalini
item.content:encoded:
Aatmiya Joshi

“We are the first generation to feel the effect of climate change and the last generation who can do something about it.”

-Barack Obama

The above lines by Barack Obama are a wake-up call to the global community to take note and contribute towards saving our environment.

To understand climate change, we should appreciate that climate is the overall pattern of temperature, humidity, wind or rainfall in a particular region. It is recorded over a long-term period. All temperature variations, changes in rainfall patterns of a place are determined by its climate.

Another question that people may have is, “What are we doing that affects our climate?” and “In what ways does it affect us?”

There are many ways in which we directly or indirectly affect our climate. For instance, when we burn fossil fuels and cut trees, carbon dioxide (CO₂) is released into the air. This gas traps all the heat reflected by the Earth’s surface and keeps it in the atmosphere. This makes the Earth warmer, which in turn affects our climate: hot places get hotter and cold places warm up which makes rainfall unpredictable, causing ice to melt and sea levels to rise.  

It may seem as if the only effect is temperature change, however, this increase in temperature has adverse effects on us in every way possible. 

When we cut trees, a lesser amount of water is sent to the atmosphere which leads to less rainfall and ultimately causes droughts in the area. We have seen unconventional rains, landslides and floods all over the world. The moisture-holding capacity of soil is reduced when trees are cut and therefore, even if it does rain, it causes floods as this water isn’t absorbed by the soil. Droughts and floods damage crops, leading to a shortage of food and causing famines. For instance, the Horn of Africa faced a terrible drought in 2020 which led to the death of 43,000 people. Moreover, due to the rise in global warming, polar ice sheets began to melt, which increased the volume of rivers, leading to higher chances of floods.

The increase in temperature causes heat strokes and dehydration. The 2022 European heatwave killed over 60,000 people. It aids in the spread of diseases like dengue and malaria to new regions. The burning of large areas of farmlands also releases smoke in the atmosphere, worsening the air quality, with the index going up to horrifying levels of 400-500, leading to choking of the surrounding population. 

The above effects of climate change disturb various sectors like farming, fishing and tourism, which impact the earnings of many people. Furthermore, repairing the damage costs trillions of dollars. 

Hotter temperatures lead to drying out of soil, causing desertification in many regions. This causes an increase in floods, soil erosion and a huge loss of fertile soil for cultivating crops.

As Barack Obama said, we are the first generation to feel and suffer from the effects of climate change. We must address this problem immediately.  

So, what can we do to protect our environment?

One way is to use the leftover space. Over time, we have cut down acres of forest areas to expand our growth. Therefore, we should use the remaining space by planting dense, fast-growing trees. These trees can be planted between roads, in societies, etc.

But what if we don’t have any space to plant these trees?

There is a solution for that too.

We can cover the flat roofs of schools, buildings, etc. with vegetation like sedums, wildflowers or small shrubs. Along with this, plants like climbing vines, ferns or spider plants can be grown on the walls of different buildings. These plants absorb heat from the sun, trap rainwater, act as insulators during winters, purify indoor air and reduce noise pollution. 

We have talked about covering buildings with plants, but what if we make eco-friendly buildings? 

The frames, ceilings and roofs of buildings are all made from concrete and steel. These materials also pollute the air. Concrete is primarily made up of cement. Cement is made by heating limestone at a high temperature, which gives limestone and carbon dioxide. To make steel, iron ore is mixed with coke and is heated, which gives out iron and carbon dioxide. The carbon dioxide formed by both reactions is released into the air; which pollutes the environment. Moreover, a lot more carbon dioxide is released during construction processes. A simple solution to this problem is using more eco-friendly materials like hempcrete. 

Hempcrete is basically a lightweight bio-composite. It is mostly made up of hemp hurds, a lime-based binder and water. Hempcrete is eco-friendly as it absorbs a lot of carbon dioxide while growing. The lime binder reacts with carbon dioxide instead of releasing it. 

In addition to this, it acts as an insulator, it is fire-proof and pest-proof and has a longer lifespan.

Now that we have found a solution for our buildings, what about our roads?

Yes, our roads also contribute towards increasing the temperatures of cities.  Most roads are made of asphalt, which is a mixture of crushed stone and bitumen. Asphalt has a deep black or dark grey colour. These roads reflect only 5% - 10% of the sunlight and absorb the rest. This makes the surface of the roads very hot. When this heat is radiated back into the air, it raises the temperature of cities by 3°- 8° C. This problem can be solved by using lighter coloured materials like gravel, which absorb up to 30% - 40% of sunlight.  Moreover, reflective coatings can be applied on the roads, which decreases the road temperatures by 10° - 15°. 

These actions are only being addressed by a handful of people, which is not enough. It requires active participation from every individual occupying the planet. Some small steps can be done by common people in their daily lives. Even though they may look like tiny steps, they can prove to be very effective. 

∙ To reduce the burning of fossil fuels and the release of carbon dioxide in the atmosphere, we must reduce our energy consumption.  This can be done by turning off fans and lights when not in use, taking in natural air and ventilation instead of using air conditioners (AC) and heaters, and by using energy-saving electronic devices.

∙ We must erase the use of plastics from our daily lives. Plastics take hundreds of years to decompose and at the same time pollute the ocean and soil. Therefore, we must use our own reusable bags and bottles instead of single-use plastics. 

∙ We must also change our modes of transportation as it plays a major role in polluting the air. Public transportation like buses can be used, or a group of people going to the same place can carpool.

∙ We must avoid the wastage of water, food and articles of common use.  This can be done by turning off taps while not in use, avoiding overuse of water in bathing, brushing and washing, and avoiding wastage of food. We can set up donation banks to help recycle articles which can be used by others. 

At an individual level, these steps can be taken by all.

I’ll conclude with a few lines from the song Heal the World by Michael Jackson:

“Heal the world

Make it a better place

For you and for me and the entire human race

There are people dying 

If you care enough for the living

Make it a better place for you and for me.”

Aatmiya Joshi is a student of class 8 at Vibgyor High Balewadi, Pune. He can be reached at aatmiyajoshi24@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Aatmiya Joshi
Climate change is both a collective crisis and responsibility. Everyday human activities like the use of air conditioning and single-use plastics have detrimental effects on the environment. Beyond highlighting the problem, the article proposes practical and scalable interventions, from green infrastructure (rooftop vegetation, eco-materials like hempcrete) to behavioral shifts in energy use, transportation, and consumption. Because meaningful climate mitigation depends on individual as well as systemic action.

item.title: All the Ds – Label or Celebrate? Difference, Diversity, or Disorder!
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Sheela Ramakrishnan

DEI (diversity, equity, and inclusion) has become such a buzzword these days! From classrooms to board rooms, society has woken up to the fact that we need to be sensitive towards all human beings. In a world that places high premium on conformity, anything or anybody out of the beaten track is viewed with a coloured lens. For most of us, diversity means bringing gender equality, pay parity, and inclusion of persons with special needs in the classroom and work place.

But a closer analysis of diversity reveals an array of features and attitudes that fall under its umbrella. The following table outlines the variety that lies within this term. A little reflection on these details and we can easily grasp the depth of awareness required and the gamut of strategies that need to be used to ensure equity and parity for all.

Of all of the above, it is quite evident that cognitive and neurodiversity are the ones that need most understanding and focus to make our classrooms truly inclusive. This is not to undermine the gravity that the other factors hold – they are certainly important and we need to be mindful of keeping ourselves bias-free in these areas. But those diversities are more visible and tangible and therefore easier to understand and be mindful about. But cognitive and neurodiversities are often unseen and do not have a strong appearance in a tangible manner. Therefore, understanding this bias better would be key to understanding diversity more completely.

Cognitive diversity: What does this look like in a classroom? Examine these scenarios.

  • Sandeep in UKG colours a mango blue. What would you, his teacher, say?
  • A class discussion on national leaders prompts conflicting responses from the students. What would you, as a social studies educator, do?
  • Jahnavi in your class is being teased by her friends as she does not use a dining table, but sits on the floor to have a meal.

What strategies we use as educators to navigate these situations will decide what kind of a classroom environment we build. Do we let our own biases creep into our transactions unknowingly or do we demonstrate that diversity is to be encouraged?

The blue mango could perhaps be one that the child saw in his dream, or in a cartoon or movie, or it could even be one that is genetically modified! But most of us, I am sure, would try to convince the child that a blue mango does not exist and that mangoes could be yellow, green, or orange! Guilty?

Different rituals or practices in different homes provide an excellent opportunity to invite sharing of various traditions and to find the benefits of such practices. Sitting on the ground aids digestion and prevents overeating. That was how people in our country ate, and still do in several homes, before we adopted the westernized table! How would you handle this in your classroom?

Can we turn conflict and debate into discussions that enable better understanding than trying to decide right from wrong? After all, there is nothing called pure black or pure white when it comes to human thought – they exist mostly in shades of grey.

The issue is do we allow for or embrace unanticipated responses from children without judgment? We need to let children know that all their opinions are valid and that we can be enriched by absorbing diverse opinions.

When schools build a culture of respecting and accepting diverse points of view and thought, we are actually sowing the seeds for a more peaceful world in the future. A world that celebrates differences. A world that respects the uniqueness of each individual mind. A world that understands that differences are what make us more human – or else we might as well all be robots or clones!

So let us make our classrooms more “thinking”, more “problem solving”, more “confidently engaged”. We need to present opportunities for students to handle different viewpoints and challenge biases. Exposure to diverse perspectives encourages young minds to come up with innovative and creative solutions. Consistent efforts in this direction develop a school culture where all students feel valued and respected. In turn, students cultivate strong and mature social emotional skills to become flexible and empathetic global citizens. They will thus be able to navigate this complex world with relative ease.

The next logical question would be: how do I help my students respect cognitive diversity?

When student and educator meet and interact in the classroom, magic can happen! Those 40 minutes of engagement decide which way the cookie crumbles. The strongest weapon that educators hold is the transaction process and attitudinal signals in the classroom. Here are some pointers:

  1. Using pedagogy or inclusive methods of transacting the curriculum, encouraging free and fair expression of differing opinions.
  2. Using structured dialogue as a strategy along with collaborative projects for free flow of ideas.
  3. Conducting culture days with stories, art, books, food, and festivals to promote better understanding.
  4. Flip your Thought can be a strategy where students are encouraged to consider a point of view in opposition to theirs. This will train them to question their own thought process and be more open-minded.
  5. Mix and match teams, for collaborative learning is a great way to get students to not only learn from each other in their own unique ways but also respect and bond with each other. This creates a holistic classroom atmosphere. Be creative while forming teams, looking to deliberately encourage diverse teams to work together towards a common goal.
  6. Build a special bank of speeches and videos on diversity among your students. Celebrate their triumphs and make your class and school truly inclusive.

Neurodivergence: Examine this situation

  1. Neha can focus on one activity only for a short period of time. Therefore, several of the assignments given in class are left incomplete. She is very bright and well-informed, but is losing confidence as most of the grades are on long assignments.
  2. Sid takes time to read the letters in his textbook and on the board, but is a smart intelligent boy when spoken to and comes up with very creative ideas for problems. However, he is lagging behind as the school grades him mainly on written assignments.
  3. Karthik prefers to keep to himself, making minimum eye contact and conversation with others. He plays the keyboard wonderfully well and is a whiz at math!

What do these children have in common? They are all neurodivergent!

Neurodivergent is a term that describes people whose brains function, learn, process, or behave in ways that differ from what is considered neurotypical or “average.” The key word here is “different” – they are neither inferior nor superior. This understanding is vital for an educator to support such students in her class.

Neha needs multiple activities to help her learn rather than one long one. Her attention span is shorter.

Sid learns better by looking at pictures rather than words. His brain processes printed letters and words differently affecting his reading and writing.

Karthik would be a great asset in a suitable project or display work as he can work by himself with minimum distraction.

A neurodivergent person is someone whose brain development and functioning diverge from societal norms in areas including:

  • Attention
  • Sensory processing
  • Social interaction
  • Communication
  • Learning patterns

Neurodiversity is an umbrella term used for persons who are neurodivergent.

Neurodiversity – neurodivergent persons

So, while persons with attention deficit or on the autism spectrum may be neurodivergent, not all persons with neurodivergence are the same.

The term “neurodiversity” was coined by sociologist Judy Singer in 1998. She explains it beautifully when she says, just as we have biodiversity on Earth, we have neurodiversity in human society. Viewed from that perspective, one could say that neurodiversity is essential for the comprehensive progress of the human race. And yet, this is one area of diversity that is not understood well and is somewhat inefficiently handled in schools. It is important to remember that Albert Einstein and Issac Newton – known for their brilliant minds – were neurodivergent on the autism spectrum.

People who are neurodivergent often experience unique emotional and behavioural symptoms, but some common indicators include:

  • Challenged with social interactions
  • Physical clumsiness
  • Increased anxiety
  • Challenged with focusing or staying still
  • Sensitivity to light, sound, touch, and smells
  • Preference for repetitive behaviours or routines

Being neurodivergent does not mean that one has poor or impaired mental health! But being neurodivergent in an unsupportive environment without adequate understanding can make one susceptible to mental health issues. But then, doesn’t that happen to everybody? Can ANYONE who lives in an unempathetic and unsupportive world, be absolutely mentally healthy? We often forget this when we deal with our students who are neurodivergent.

How can I support my neurodivergent students and build an inclusive classroom is the question that crops up in every educator’s mind.

Some key strategies are:

  1. Understanding and building awareness of the various expressions of neurodivergence and knowing when to seek help in identifying them. Being aware and informed, attending workshops and seeking expert opinion is key.
  2. Upskilling oneself in providing support to those neurodivergent students who are in a mainstream class. Championing their cause with the school for suitable resources – human and material.
  3. Building an Inclusive environment in your class by using neutral language and avoiding labelling. Eg: “hello students, class, team, or children” instead of “boys and girls”, “child who is adopted” rather than “an adopted child” “person who has autism” instead of “autistic child” “person on a wheelchair” rather than “wheel chair bound”.
  4. Focus on the strengths of the students, like pattern recognition, big picture view, creativity, and what they CAN do rather than not. Set realistic goals for them to achieve.
  5. Make environmental changes in an inconspicuous manner to help those with sensory sensitivities. It could be light and sound levels or even seating changes that could make the child more comfortable and thereby be able to focus on learning.
  6. Help build support groups for the children as well as the parents. This will help normalize the situation, build communities and validate their experiences.

Introducing neurodivergence in an age-appropriate sensitive manner to students also forms part of building an inclusive classroom. One needs to tailor conversations and support the child’s developmental stage. Understanding the uniqueness of the wiring of the brain rather than its deficiencies will help smoothen progress from concrete sensory to abstract executive function skills in teenagers.

  • Ages 2 to 5: Focus on concrete, sensory-based language. Explain that some like loud noises and some prefer quiet – everyone’s brain works differently.
  • Ages 6 to 11: help them understand how their own brain is different, introduce the term and treat it as each one having their own strengths and challenges. Motivate them to self-advocate fearlessly.
  • Ages 12+: Understanding executive function skills, self-awareness and managing emotions. Discuss how young frontal brains are still developing up to the age of 25 and the connections in the brain work differently for different people. Sometimes neurodivergent people take time to manage higher level thinking and impulsivity.

In my opinion, the most important of all – whatever be the type of diversity – is what is the bias that I hold within myself as an educator? Do I view diversity as a difference to be celebrated or a disorder to be labelled? The answer to this lies only and only within each one of us. May we have the courage to look within, find the answer, and take action.

Here are two real life stories, long before the term neurodivergent became popular. Names and details are changed to protect privacy.

Different, yet gifted!

The Year, 1960s… a young mother, married to an elite bureaucrat’s family, realized that her son was different. The words spastic, high function (for the slightly less than perfect left forearm and gait), and dyslexic, did not make any sense to her! All she knew was that her child needed help and support. Armed with nothing but her conviction and defying family traditional stereotypes of what a woman can do, she stuck to her resolve. Her greatest strength was that she was good at English, something not so common in those days. She went from one library to another (no Google baba then) trying to absorb as much as she could about her son’s condition. She learnt strategies, implemented them painstakingly, one at a time. She went with her child to various institutions that were ill-equipped to support the child, yet she never gave up the search for a place where he would be accepted. After several attempts, she decided to home school him herself while continuing her efforts. She consulted experts, built support groups (imagine how difficult it would have been without technology), and finally succeeded in motivating the setting up of the first institution in the city, for those who had dyslexia! She remained in the background, never coming to the forefront.

She took her son for physiotherapy in the hope that the forearm would become “normal.” Little did she know that it was a gift that the boy came with, waiting to be discovered. And discovered it was – the boy was obsessed with cricket! So, she decided to nurture his interest, and he turned out to be a lethal bowler as his forearm naturally lent itself to those googly balls! She decided to follow the path less taken and stood staunchly by him to let him choose cricket as a vocation. With dyslexia not letting him function well in mainstream school, he completed his schooling privately while pursuing cricket.

All that hard work, sweat, heart break and tears paid off when he was selected for the regional team! He made his team and sponsors proud. He got employed by a regional corporate firm under the sports quota. With newfound confidence and all the therapy that he had undergone earlier, coupled with his mother’s relentless support, he completed his graduation. The rest of the family began to see him with new eyes. He won the respect of his colleagues at the work place with his simplicity and hard work, making his sponsors proud at every match!

Life smiled on him when he got married to a smart, intelligent, compassionate girl from a slightly lower economic background than theirs. An arranged marriage that was founded on mutual respect and not pity. The new daughter-in-law quickly upskilled herself, learnt the ways of the elite world, without losing her roots, became a productive earning member while building a warm and loving family. They soon became parents to a son. As the child grew, and the internet explosion happened, loads of information about his father’s condition became available. He grew up with a sound value system based on inclusion as a way of life than as a favour.

Today, Deepak is leading a peaceful retired life. His son, Dev, is pursuing his doctorate from Europe. His wife Seema continues to work at a senior level in the disability area. Deepak’s mother, now almost 90, watches the fruit of all her labour with quiet satisfaction and joy.

No headlines, no fame, no awards – just an acceptance of diversity, of saying I am different, but not less, of love and care, and above all, respect for one another. And a mother, who wouldn’t give up!

Different – supported to rise!

Mamta was a confused parent. She was almost raising Avinash by herself with her spouse mostly absent due to work demands. She realized that certain milestones that Avinash had to reach regarding gross and fine motor movements were not like other kids. He had difficulty in using the jungle gym, or the slide, or even tying shoe laces. Otherwise, he was a happy child.

More issues surfaced as he began school. Reading and writing began to pose challenges. Be it letters or numbers, Avinash could not navigate his classroom tasks. However, his speech and general interactions were all normal and he was well-liked by his peers and teachers. But everybody threw up their hands when it came to regular academics. There was general frustration as none of the schools in the city where they lived had either an answer or a solution. He was being shunted from one school to another in the hope that there would be some place where he would fit in. It was the late 90s, when learning differences were hardly understood. Such children were labelled. Teachers were lost as they didn’t understand what they were dealing with. Assessments were undertaken at various centres over several years – and eventually after much waiting, the verdict was pronounced. Avinash had dyslexia, dyscalculia, and dyspraxia - all forms of neurodivergence! It was not easy to accept and even more challenging to think of the next steps.

Mamta knew that our country did not yet have an institution that could support her child’s learning, given this situation. She did what mothers do – turned her life upside down for her child. She sought a transfer from her work place to an overseas destination that had institutions that specially supported neurodivergent learning. That she could do it was the silver lining.

Avinash blossomed and began to grow with a positive identity rather than being weighed down by the labels he had worn earlier. He carried on to another overseas destination for his undergraduate studies. His creative thinking mind and high interpersonal skills coupled with being tech savvy became his strengths. With support from the system, he earned his business degree and then went on to complete a digital marketing programme. Over the years, with care and support and adaptations, he overcame the limitations of dyspraxia.

No prizes for guessing what happened next. Avinash now about 30, a strapping handsome man, is a marketing professional, working in India, married to his childhood sweetheart and leading a joyful life. He was a man who was fortunate to get the required support, as he had the backing of adequate resources at that time. But that is only one part of the story.

The main takeaway from the stories of Deepak and Avinash is that neurodivergence is NOT a disability – it is a difference that needs to be supported and celebrated. Our world is a more talented, colourful and compassionate place because of persons like them – and despite “normal” persons like us!

You and I , Us and They , there is only One Way
The two sides drew their breath short
Armoured, weaponed and most importantly, furious
Today was the day
This was the moment.
The battle to the finish line
The struggle for supremacy
To its last cannon.
Unmindful of bloodshed, loss, grief and death.
The bullets rained , the drones crashed, the debris flew, straight into the hearts of those who came in the way.
Borders blurred while the ripples of aggression began to leave its footprints.

Man may think he is an island and it’s his world vs their world, but nature in her own way shows that we are one.
Woe that we don’t see the invisible thread that binds us.
We see divisions, of thoughts, opinion, race, and colour. Of power, influence, politics and ulterior motives.
Will we ever learn? That Acceptance of Diversity is the Only way. There is NO other.
Stop searching. Embrace Reality before further Calamity.

Sheela Ramakrishnan is an author, educational consultant, and counsellor based in Hyderabad. She can be reached at sheelaforlearn@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Sheela Ramakrishnan
Our education systems often tend to prioritize conformity. We have the instinct to categorize and label students, but we are forgetting that diversity is an essential trait in the classroom; furthermore, it’s a beautiful aspect of human existence – much like the biodiversity in nature. This piece advocates for systemic change via teacher training, inclusive environments, and conscious language use.

item.title: A hands-on exploration of plane mirrors and lateral inversion
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Vajahat Zehra

Plane mirrors is a fascinating topic encountered in middle-school science. Students generally engage enthusiastically with this chapter, as it allows them to explore various concepts through hands-on activities, such as working with mirrors, kaleidoscopes, and pinhole cameras.

While teaching, employing a constructivist approach has proven effective in helping students grasp the concept. Below, I share insights into how I teach the properties of plane mirrors with particular focus on addressing the challenges of lateral inversion.

Key properties of plane mirrors

Through guided exploration, students independently discover three fundamental properties of plane mirrors:

  1. The size of the image in the mirror is identical to the size of the object.
  2. The distance of the image from the mirror is equal to the distance of the object from the mirror.
  3. The image is laterally inverted as noted in various textbooks.

While the first two properties are relatively straightforward for students to grasp, the concept of lateral inversion often poses challenges. To address this, I developed a series of activities that make the concept more tangible and accessible.

Addressing the challenge of lateral inversion

Early in my teaching career, I noticed that students struggled to understand lateral inversion when asked to draw mirror images of their names. Some drew vertical mirror lines, while others drew horizontal ones, leading to confusion about the correct orientation of the reflected image. To overcome this, I introduced a modified activity, where students had to position a mirror at various angles and observe the resulting images. This approach allows students to explore how the orientation of the mirror affects the reflection.

To further reinforce the concept, I incorporated hands-on tools such as Rubik's cubes and 3D letters from the English alphabet. By manipulating these objects, students could observe how lateral inversion occurs. Using graph paper, they were able to accurately illustrate and measure the size and position of the images, ensuring a deeper understanding of the relationship between the object and its reflection.

Understanding lateral inversion

Lateral inversion refers to the phenomenon whereby an object appears reversed when viewed in a mirror. For instance, the letter "b" appears as "d" in its mirror image. A ray diagram illustrates that the image produced by a plane mirror is laterally inverted; however, students may misunderstand that right and left have been swapped. This common misconception arises from our interpretation of the image rather than an actual reversal. In reality, mirrors perform a front-to-back inversion. The part of the object that is farthest from the mirror appears most distant in the reflection, while the closest part remains at the forefront.

Educators should emphasize that the distance between the object and its image in a plane mirror is always equal, a property that can be leveraged to enhance students' understanding of reflection.

Teaching strategies

To make the concept of lateral inversion more accessible, the following strategies are recommended:

Hands-on exploration with everyday objects

Provide students with a variety of objects, such as solved Rubik's cubes, letters of the English alphabet, dice, and book covers. Ask them to observe and compare the objects with their mirror images. This activity helps students identify two key properties: that the size of the image matches the size of the object, and the distance of the image from the mirror equals the distance of the object from the mirror.

Guided discovery through manipulation

Encourage students to determine whether the image is identical to that in the mirror or whether lateral inversion has occurred. By manipulating objects in different orientations, students will discover that the image is not always laterally inverted; it depends on the distance of each point of the object from the mirror.

Visualizing lateral inversion

Use a variety of objects to help students visualize lateral inversion. For example, ask them to write their names or draw shapes and then observe how these appear in the mirror. This activity helps students understand that mirrors do not simply swap right and left but invert the front and back of the object.

Interactive drawing exercises

Provide students with graph papers and ask them to draw mirror images of letters, shapes, or patterns. Encourage them to experiment with both vertical and horizontal mirror orientations. This exercise reinforces the concept of lateral inversion and helps students develop spatial reasoning skills.

Integration with mathematics

Connect the concept of lateral inversion to symmetry in mathematics. Discuss how mirror images relate to symmetrical shapes and patterns. This interdisciplinary approach not only reinforces scientific concepts but also enriches students' mathematical understanding.

Conclusion

By employing interactive and engaging strategies, educators can make the concept of lateral inversion more accessible to middle school students, thereby enhancing their comprehension of how light behaves and reflects.

Vajahat Zehra is a higher secondary chemistry teacher with over 20 years of teaching experience. Currently based in Mumbai, she is passionate about nurturing curiosity and developing a strong interest in science and chemistry among her students. She believes in using diverse teaching strategies, particularly constructive pedagogy, where students take ownership of their learning. Through this approach, learners actively explore concepts in depth, ask questions, and build their own understanding of the subject. She can be reached at vajahat.zehra@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Vajahat Zehra
A lesson on mirrors and reflection makes a middle-school classroom come alive. Students move from confusion to clarity as they engage in hands-on experiments with everyday objects like Rubik’s cubes and letters. Additionally, the shift from passive instruction to guided exploration helps students uncover key properties of reflection on their own.

item.title: What today’s youth are teaching the rest of us
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Neerja Singh

Imagine a world where teenagers rehearse job interviews with digital avatars, master professional skills on online platforms rather than in classrooms, and push schools to take mental wellbeing seriously. This is not science fiction. It is the everyday reality of young people growing up in 2026. Members of Generation Z (born roughly between 1997-2012) and Generation Alpha (born roughly between 2013-2025) are redefining how identity, education, work, and purpose are understood. Raised in a world shaped by artificial intelligence, global connectivity, and constant change, they are moving away from rigid life paths and designing more fluid, personalized journeys.

For many parents, teachers, and employers, this shift can feel unsettling. Earlier generations followed clearer scripts: study hard, earn a degree, secure a stable job, and gradually climb the ladder. These expectations reflect the experiences of Generation X (born between 1965-1980) and Millennials (born between 1981-1996), whose lives were shaped by more structured systems of education and employment. But young people entering adulthood today are quietly rewriting these rules. Instead of fixed routes, they are experimenting with new combinations of technology, skills, values, and wellbeing.

One of the most visible changes lies in how they relate to technology. For earlier generations, digital tools were occasional assistants. For Gen Z and Gen Alpha, technology, especially artificial intelligence, is woven into everyday thinking. Artificial intelligence is not simply a software that performs tasks; it is increasingly becoming a partner in exploration and decision-making. Students now use AI tools to explore career options, practise interviews, brainstorm ideas, and analyze possibilities they might never have considered otherwise.

A university student who once planned a career in finance may experiment with an AI-based career tool only to have his eyes opened to emerging opportunities in healthcare analytics, prompting him to rethink his entire path. Stories like this are becoming increasingly common across India. Even school students are beginning to interact confidently with intelligent systems, practising mock interviews with AI avatars that simulate recruiters. Instead of memorizing textbook responses, they experiment with different answers, receive feedback, and refine their communication. Teachers who incorporate such tools in their teaching often notice that students become more confident and reflective. In the workplace too, companies increasingly expect young recruits to work alongside AI tools to generate ideas, analyze information, and improve their efficiency. For older generations, the idea that machines might influence life choices can feel unsettling, yet for many young people, these technologies simply expand the horizon of possibilities. The real question for parents and educators is no longer whether AI should exist in young people’s lives – it already does – but how to guide its thoughtful use.

At the same time, a deeper transformation is unfolding in how young people view education and success. For decades, degrees and job titles served as the primary markers of achievement. Today’s youth still value education, but they increasingly emphasize skills over credentials alone. Many believe that the ability to learn continuously and apply practical knowledge matters more than the prestige of a particular degree. This shift is visible across India, where young people are building careers through skill development, online learning, and project-based experience. A young man who struggled with conventional academic exams might discover that he excels in coding or digital design, while another might learn online marketing and build a small e-commerce venture that supports his family. Such journeys reflect a broader movement toward portfolio-based learning. Instead of relying on a single qualification, many young professionals now assemble portfolios of projects, certifications, and real-world work. Employers are slowly adapting to this change as well, with technology firms and start-ups increasingly looking for demonstrable skills such as completed projects, digital portfolios, or open-source contributions rather than only academic marks.

Equally significant is a shift in motivation. Earlier generations often aspired to long-term promotions and senior titles. Many young professionals today place greater value on growth, learning, and meaningful work. Flexibility and creativity often matter as much as salary or designation, and continuous learning has become part of professional life as many young people regularly upgrade their skills in response to rapid technological change.

Yet, ambition is only one part of the story. Another defining feature of this generation is a growing commitment to mental wellbeing. Young people today speak far more openly about stress, burnout, and emotional health than previous generations did. Academic pressure, social comparison, and digital overload can weigh heavily on adolescents, and increasingly, young people insist that success should not come at the cost of mental health. In schools and colleges, students are beginning to advocate for healthier environments by initiating peer-support groups, organizing wellbeing days, or encouraging open conversations about stress, failure, and resilience. These initiatives are not acts of defiance but reflections of a growing awareness that emotional balance is essential for sustained performance and creativity. Families are gradually adjusting as well. Parents who once equated discipline solely with academic achievement are beginning to recognize the importance of emotional support. When young people feel heard and understood, they often develop stronger motivation and resilience. In this sense, the emphasis on mental health represents not weakness but a new form of strength, one that recognizes human limits in an increasingly demanding world.

Another transformation lies in how young people relate to authority and institutions. Traditional hierarchies, whether in schools, workplaces, or families, are giving way to more collaborative models. Young people increasingly expect to participate in shaping the systems that affect them and want their voices heard in classrooms, offices, and communities. In educational settings, this might mean involving students in discussions about policies, activities, or campus life. When students feel ownership, engagement rises significantly. Workplaces are also experimenting with new forms of collaboration, including “reverse mentoring”, where younger employees share insights on technology, culture, and emerging trends with senior leaders. This collaborative instinct reflects the participatory culture of the digital world. Social media platforms have accustomed young people to expressing opinions, building communities, and contributing ideas, and as a result, they often expect similar openness in offline institutions. Organizations that encourage dialogue and co-creation tend to connect more effectively with younger generations.

Finally, perhaps the most striking characteristic of today’s youth is their attempt to balance idealism with practicality. Many care deeply about issues such as environmental sustainability, social equity, and ethical business practices, yet they are also acutely aware of economic realities and the need for stable livelihoods. This combination of purpose and pragmatism is shaping new kinds of careers and enterprises. Young entrepreneurs are exploring business models that combine financial sustainability with social or environmental impact, developing technology solutions for climate challenges, creating platforms that support local communities or promoting responsible consumption. These efforts reflect a generation that refuses to separate profit from responsibility. Young professionals want their work to align with their values, but they also recognize the importance of building viable careers.

Taken together, these shifts – AI-assisted identities, skill-based learning, attention to mental health, collaborative culture, and values-driven ambition – show how profoundly the youth landscape is changing. These are not temporary trends but responses to a world defined by rapid technological change, economic uncertainty, and global conflicts and challenges such as climate change. For parents, educators, and leaders, the task is not to resist these changes but to understand them. Every generation develops its own ways of navigating the realities of its time, and today’s youth are learning to adapt to a future that is less predictable than ever before.

India, with its unique blend of cultural tradition and technological transformation, stands at a particularly interesting moment. The wisdom of older generations, discipline, perseverance, and community values remain deeply important, while the adaptability and creativity of younger generations offer fresh ways of responding to complex challenges. When these perspectives meet with mutual respect, generational differences can become sources of insight rather than conflict. Listening carefully to the aspirations and anxieties of young people may therefore be the most important step forward. When society moves beyond rigid scripts and begins to co-create its future, a more resilient and inclusive world begins to take shape.

Neerja Singh is a generational diversity speaker, author of six generational books, and a generational coach. A TEDx speaker and certified virtual presenter, she assists multinational companies, educational institutions, and the armed forces to bridge generational gaps. An Oxford University Press Resource Person, she is pursuing a Senior Management Executive Program at IIM Trichy. She can be reached at https://www.linkedin.com/in/neerja-singh/.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Neerja Singh
Today’s youth are breaking away from the structured life scripts followed by previous generations. Coming of age in an era defined by technology and constant change, Generation Z and Alpha are not only rehearsing interviews with AI avatars and prioritizing their mental health, but shifting from obtaining degrees to refining their skills. This piece offers a nuanced understanding of how these changes are helping them adapt to a far more complex and uncertain world.

item.title: What it means to be “in your element”
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Timira

Mamta, a 25-year-old sprightly, single mother to a 6-year-old, walks into my house every morning at 10:30 with her beaming smile and infectious enthusiasm asking, “Didi, aaj kya banau?” This, after having finished cooking in three households before mine. Mamta cooks about 5-6 dishes, from prepping to washing up and leaving the kitchen squeaky clean – all in less than an hour. I often watch her in the kitchen. It is like watching a beautifully choreographed piece of performance. Everything is laid out, ingredients, tools, vessels. The two gas burners are put to work immediately while her hands are busy mixing, kneading, chopping in a way that not a minute is wasted. While something is marinating, there’s a dish cooking. While something is cooking, she’s chopping for the next dish. And while things are simmering, she’s washing up and answering her daughter’s phone call to let her know that she’ll be home soon. Her movements around the kitchen carry the ease and grace of an artist. When Mamta is in the kitchen, she is truly “in her element”!

So, what does it mean to be in your element? When was the last time you felt you were “in your element”? What were you doing at the time? It could be that history class in which you had every single child engaged and hooked and you just knew how you had cracked that class! Or it could be the time you single-handedly organized a huge family celebration and felt completely chuffed at its success.

Ken Robinson does an excellent job breaking down this idea in his book titled The Element – How Finding Your Passion Changes Everything. He says:

“When people are in their Element, they connect with something fundamental to their sense of identity, purpose, and well-being. Being there provides a sense of self-revelation, of defining who they really are and what they’re really meant to be doing with their lives. The Element is the meeting point between natural aptitude and personal passion.”

I took the liberty to draw it out in the Venn diagram below:

When personal and professional unite

Most teachers sit in this sweet intersection above. But it could also be a spot in which we can find ourselves stuck. Teachers have a professional identity attached to being ‘passionate’ about the work we do. Sometimes that passion gets chipped away, but we continue doing the work under the pretense of loving what we do. The reason why I am writing about this in my column that focuses on the ‘Teacher Within’ is for us to recognize that we are dynamic individuals with expansive interests, capacities and potentials, and the attempt here is to find different ways to keep engaging with ourselves and our varied passions, get closer to our authentic selves and slowly and skillfully erase the lines drawn between who we are as individuals and who we identify as professionals in the classroom. How can our individual selves feed our professional selves and how can we take what we learn as professionals into our personal lives?

Teachers have often been told to leave their personal lives at the school gate. I tend to disagree because it makes teachers inauthentic and pushes them away from being ‘in their element’. It is deeply important for young people to experience authenticity when they engage with adults in their lives.

I get it. I love it. I WANT it. But HOW?

We all have a natural aptitude towards certain things and usually these aptitudes are recognized when we are young. I remember my brother was someone who loved pulling things apart and putting them back together – the radio, the Walkman we shared, my talking doll, parts of his cycle. While I was always creating things that didn’t exist – new board games, new ways of eating the same old food, handmade notebooks with all kinds of different pages, I could go on. Children explore their natural aptitude quite organically; the more they explore, the more they develop their skills; and as they get better at it, they begin to enjoy it and love it. However, as we grow into adulthood, we begin to lose touch with our natural aptitudes and tend to work towards developing skills that seem to be ‘beneficial’ to us. It’s probably why the world is full of adults who are perhaps good at what they do but they don’t really love doing it. What is wonderful about our aptitudes is that we may have several over our lifetime and exploring them allows us to engage with our growing and changing selves in intimate ways.

Robinson, in his book, adds two interesting aspects that could aid in finding our Element – attitude and opportunity. I’d like to bring Mamta back here. At 25, she already knows that even though she loves cooking and is proud of what she puts out every day, she’s not going to love this all her life. She wants to train to be a make-up artist. At this point, she doesn’t know if she has an aptitude for it, but her passion is enough for her to pursue it. She’s been attending night school all of last year and is giving her class 10 board exams as I write this article. Why? Because the course she wants to join requires her to complete class 12, so she’s determined to give it another two years and complete her education in order to pursue this. In addition, it’s an expensive programme, so she’s been taking puranpoli and modak orders to make the extra income she needs for the course. This is the attitude that Robinson talks about.

“Attitude is our personal perspective on our selves and our circumstances – our angle on things, our disposition, and emotional point of view. Many things affect our attitudes, including our basic character, our spirit, our sense of self-worth, the perceptions of those around us, and their expectations of us.”

“How we perceive our circumstances and how we create and take opportunities depends largely on what we expect of ourselves.”

I draw immense strength and inspiration from women like Mamta who help us examine what our relationship with ourselves and our expectations of ourselves is. How much our view of our self is influenced or determined by others’ expectations and perceptions of us? Remember the year you got a subject you didn’t know much about and felt very incompetent to teach it? Or a school event which you were pushed into that was totally out of your forte? You were caught in that place where you love what you do, but suddenly, you weren’t very good at it. Try to recall what you did to overcome that?

I remember asking a pre-school teacher to teach science to grade 3 for one semester. She had to teach the topic on birds and confessed she didn’t know much beyond the local crow, pigeon, and house sparrow. Well, she could have just opened the textbook and taught what was in there, but Ms. K was a teacher who liked being in her element. Weekend mornings were spent going for bird walks; she joined every forum there was about local Mumbai birds, brought out her old dusty binoculars, picked out all the bird books from the school library and within weeks, she realized she was a deeply passionate bird enthusiast and had never quite known this about herself!

Another teacher, a complete non-reader, joined the reading programme in school and discovered a world of solitude in books.
