She said she always found reading boring as a child, but suddenly here she was realizing that books created a world for her that helped her switch off from a hectic school day and relieve her from her demanding joint family. Today, she’s part of a reading club in Mumbai that spends a few hours every week reading amidst Nature, within the city, together, but in silence.

I’ll never forget the teacher suffering from severe insomnia, who decided to fight her doctors and family by refusing medication and instead trusted the arts to help her body reset her sleeping pattern. As part of the Artful teacher workshop programme, she picked up Mandala drawing as her Personal Creative Practice and spent her waking hours of the night drawing mandalas which relaxed her nerves, brought down her anxiety around sleep, and after several months of practice, it eventually helped her get her sleep back.

Get to know the teacher within

Being in your element depends a lot on your relationship with yourself. Take time to build a deep relationship with yourself. Spend time alone. Do things on your own. Learn new things. Explore new interests. Step out of your comfort zone. Don’t judge yourself or your age! My friend’s 65-year-old Malayali mother decided she wanted to learn German one day and travelled across the city twice a week to learn alongside a class full of 20-year-olds. She didn’t find her element in the German language; she perhaps found her element in the act of showing up for herself!

New opportunities can help us teachers understand that our identity is not limited to what we teach and how we teach it. It is connected to who we are as individuals, how we engage with the world, what moves us, and what our deepest passions are. This will feed into who we are and expand our own perceptions of our self. When these aspects of life come alive, they will find their way into the classroom in ways that may surprise us at first, but soon become an unconscious part of an authentic self we learn to carry with us wherever we go.

Are you listening to you?

We are all beings who are constantly growing, and it is imperative to continuously engage with our self to get to know what the current version is like. Let’s not take our likes and dislikes, comforts and discomforts for granted. If you were terrible at drawing as a child, it doesn’t mean you still are. The more we engage with our self, the more we begin to trust our own intuition. A sense we know so little about. Yet, as teachers we use it so much to make micro decisions all the time. Tagore strongly believed that a teacher must be guided by their gut. I’d like to end with a piece of his advice to one of his teachers, “Do not be preoccupied with method. Leave your instincts to guide you to life. Children differ from one another. One must learn to know them, to navigate among them as one navigates among reefs. To explore the geography of their minds, a mysterious instinct, sympathetic to life, is the best of all guides.”

When we doubt ourselves, we feel unsure about decisions that come out of our intuition. And we doubt our intuition when we stop engaging with ourselves in intimate truthful ways. Being one with our instinct is a wonderful way to be in our element.

Timira is an arts-based therapist, educator and children’s author. She has been working in the field of education for the past 16 years designing arts-based curriculum and working with teachers. She currently co-designs the Artful Teacher Workshop Progamme and is pursuing her PhD in teacher education. She can be reached at TeachersAsArtistsCollective@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Timira
When do we truly feel like ourselves? As adults, we often drift away from our natural aptitudes in our pursuit of practical and stable careers. What if the key to fulfillment lies not in doing more, but in rediscovering what already lights us up? But reconnecting with our forgotten interests brings us back “in our element”, an ongoing relationship with oneself, not a fixed destination.

item.title: The language of mathematics: building vocabulary across grades
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Monica Kochar

Eight-year-old Meera from my grade 3 classroom had a fascinating habit. During math lessons, she would solve problems correctly, but describe her thinking in the most creative ways. Instead of saying, "I subtracted," she'd say, "I took away some numbers." Rather than "multiply," she'd say, "I made the number bigger by adding it to itself many times."

Her mathematical thinking was absolutely sound, but she lacked the precise vocabulary that would help her communicate with other mathematicians. This got me thinking: When do we introduce mathematical language? How do we help students across grades 1 to 12 develop fluency in speaking, reading, and writing mathematics?

My time with Meera sparked a journey that changed how I think about mathematical communication.

Mathematics as a living language

We often think of mathematics as numbers and symbols, but it's fundamentally a language, a way of describing relationships, patterns, and logical thinking. Just like Hindi or English, mathematical language can be acquired through listening, speaking, reading, and writing.

The challenge in our Indian context is that students encounter mathematical concepts in multiple languages simultaneously. They might think in their mother tongue (Tamil, Bengali, Gujarati), learn in Hindi or English at school, and see symbols that represent universal mathematical ideas. This linguistic richness can be an asset if we approach it thoughtfully.

Research shows that students who can articulate their mathematical thinking clearly demonstrate deeper understanding and better problem-solving abilities (Pimm, 1987). When grade 2 students can explain why 5 + 3 = 8, they're not just showing that they know the answer, they're revealing their understanding of addition as combining different quantities.

The early years: mathematical storytelling (classes LKG-2)

In the earliest grades, mathematical language emerges naturally through stories, songs, and conversations. Five-year-old Aarav in my LKG class didn't say, "A triangle has three sides." Instead, he'd say, "This shape has three pointy corners like a mountain."

This natural, descriptive language is perfect for young learners. Our job isn't to correct it, but to expand it.

I learned to become a mathematical language interpreter, helping children connect their intuitive descriptions with formal mathematical vocabulary:

  • Child says: "This number is bigger."
    Teacher adds: "Yes, 7 is greater than 5."

  • Child says: "I counted them all together."
    Teacher adds: "You found the sum! 3 plus 4 equals 7."

  • Child says: "I shared them equally."
    Teacher adds: "You divided 8 into 2 equal groups."

The key is additive language building, never replacing their natural expressions but enriching them with mathematical precision.

Primary years: building fluency (classes 3-5)

By grade 3, students like Meera are ready for more systematic mathematical vocabulary development. This is when we bridge the gap between informal mathematical thinking and formal mathematical communication.

I discovered that the most effective approach was what I call "mathematical translation practice." Students would solve problems and then explain their solutions in three ways:

  1. In their own words (informal mathematical language).
  2. Using mathematical vocabulary (formal terms).
  3. With symbols and numbers (mathematical notation).

For example, when working with fractions in grade 4, students might describe the same concept as:

  • "I cut the circle into 4 equal pieces and took 3 pieces."
  • "I found three-fourths of the circle."
  • "I calculated 3/4 of the whole."

This approach honoured their natural mathematical thinking while building technical vocabulary systematically.

The vocabulary wall strategy

In grades 3-5, I created interactive vocabulary walls that grew throughout the year. Unlike static word lists, these walls became living documents of our mathematical conversations.

  • Red words: New vocabulary we're learning this week.
  • Blue words: Words we use confidently.
  • Green words: Words we can teach younger students.

Students loved moving words from red to blue to green as their confidence grew. Ten-year-old Rahul in grade 5 became our "fraction expert" and would regularly teach grade 3 students about "numerator" and "denominator".

Middle school: academic language development (classes 6-8)

Grade 6 brings new challenges. Students encounter abstract concepts like algebra, where mathematical language becomes crucial for understanding. The word "variable" isn't just vocabulary, it represents a fundamental mathematical idea that students must grasp to progress.

Twelve-year-old Priya struggled with algebra until I helped her connect the English word "variable" with its mathematical meaning. "Think of it as something that varies, changes," I explained. "Like the weather varies from day to day, x can vary to represent different numbers."

In middle school, I focus on:

Mathematical reading skills: Students learn to read word problems like literature, identifying key mathematical vocabulary and relationships.

Mathematical writing: Grade 7 students write mathematical explanations, justifying their solutions using precise language.

Mathematical discussion: Grade 8 students engage in structured mathematical discourse, using sentence frames like "I agree with your solution because..." or "I solved it differently by..."

High school: precision and proof (classes 9-12)

By grade 9, mathematical language becomes the foundation for formal reasoning and proof. Students must be able to distinguish between "similar" and "congruent" triangles, understand why "correlation" doesn't imply "causation," and communicate complex mathematical arguments clearly.

Fifteen-year-old Arjun in grade 10 initially struggled with geometry proofs until I helped him see them as mathematical stories. "Every proof is an argument," I explained. "You're convincing someone that something must be true, step by logical step."

We practiced writing mathematical arguments like legal cases:

  • Claim: What are we trying to prove?
  • Evidence: What mathematical facts support our claim?
  • Reasoning: Why does the evidence lead to our conclusion?

Multilingual mathematical minds

One of the most beautiful aspects of teaching mathematics in India is our multilingual context. In my grade 4 classroom in Mumbai, students often made breakthrough connections when they could connect mathematical concepts across languages.

Kavya understood fractions better when she connected the Hindi word "bhag" (part) with the English concept of "fraction." Ravi grasped the concept of "perimeter" when I connected it to the Marathi word "parishee" (boundary).

Rather than seeing multilingualism as a barrier, I learned to use it as a bridge. Students would:

  • Learn mathematical concepts in their mother tongue first.
  • Connect these concepts to mathematical vocabulary in English.
  • Practice using both languages to explain their thinking.

The magic of mathematical conversations

The most powerful vocabulary development happened during mathematical conversations. When I changed from asking "What's the answer?" to "How did you think about this problem?", everything changed.

Students began using phrases like:

  • "First, I noticed that..."
  • "This reminds me of when we..."
  • "I disagree because..."
  • "Another way to think about it is..."

These conversation stems became the foundation for mathematical thinking across all grades.

Assessment through language

Traditional mathematics assessment focuses on getting the correct answers. But when we assess mathematical language, we gain insights into student thinking that numbers alone can't provide.

I began asking students to:

  • Explain their reasoning in writing.
  • Create word problems using specific vocabulary.
  • Teach younger students mathematical concepts.
  • Critique solutions using mathematical language.

These language-rich assessments revealed understanding that multiple-choice tests missed entirely.

Building mathematical identity through language

When students develop mathematical language, they develop mathematical identity. They begin to see themselves as people who can think mathematically, communicate mathematically, and belong to the mathematical community.

Nine-year-old Sneha in grade 4 transformed from a quiet student who rarely spoke to someone who would confidently explain her problem-solving strategies to the entire class. "When I can explain my thinking," she told me, "I know I really understand it."

Practical implementation across grades

The beauty of mathematical language development is that it requires no special materials or technology, just intentional teaching practices:

Daily mathematical discussions: Start each lesson with 5 minutes of mathematical conversation.

Think-pair-share: Students discuss mathematical ideas with their partners before sharing them with the class.

Mathematical journals: Students write about their mathematical thinking regularly.

Vocabulary games: Make learning mathematical terms engaging through word games and activities.

Real-world connections: Use mathematical language to describe everyday situations.

Quick resources you can use

For primary teachers (classes LKG-5):

  • Translation practice: Have students explain solutions in their own words, then mathematical language, then symbols.
  • Vocabulary walls: Create interactive displays where words "graduate" from new to confident to teachable.
  • Mathematical storytelling: Encourage students to create stories using mathematical vocabulary.

For middle school teachers (classes 6-8):

  • Sentence frames: Provide structures like "I solved this by..." or "This is similar to... because..."
  • Mathematical debates: Organize discussions where students argue for different solution methods using precise language.
  • Cross-language connections: Help students connect mathematical concepts across languages they know.

For high school teachers (classes 9-12):

  • Proof writing: Teach mathematical arguments as structured reasoning with claim, evidence, and logical steps.
  • Peer explanation: Have students explain complex concepts to each other using academic mathematical language.
  • Real-world applications: Connect abstract mathematical language to concrete professional and career contexts.

Remember: Mathematical language develops through use, not memorization. Create opportunities for students to speak, read, and write mathematically every day.

References

  1. Pimm, D. (1987). Speaking mathematically: Communication in mathematics classrooms. Routledge.
  2. Schleppegrell, M. J. (2007). The linguistic challenges of mathematics teaching and learning: A research review. Reading & Writing Quarterly, 23(2), 139-159.
  3. Kochar, M. (2023). Reflect on Dewey's (1938). HumaneMaths. Retrieved from https://humanemaths.blogspot.com/
  4. Moschkovich, J. (2002). A situated and sociocultural perspective on bilingual mathematics learners. Mathematical Thinking and Learning, 4(2&3), 189-212.
  5. National Council of Teachers of Mathematics. (2014). Principles to actions: Ensuring mathematical success for all. NCTM.

Monica Kochar is a seasoned educator and curriculum designer specializing in math education. With over 10 years of experience, she created HumaneMath, a human-centric math program, and has worked with leading organizations in edtech, publishing, and training. Her expertise spans curriculum design, teacher training, assessments, and quality review. Monica has contributed to key startups, including WhiteHat Jr., and mentored several initiatives. She holds a Master’s in Education from the University of the People. She can be reached at humanemath@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Monica Kochar
We often reduce mathematics to symbols and formulas, overlooking its true nature as a language of patterns, relationships, and logic. An example is an 8-year-old who solves math problems flawlessly but describes them in her own inventive language – “taking away numbers” instead of subtracting. This article reframes math education and shows how vocabulary development is central to understanding mathematical concepts.

item.title: A language we can dream in: the promise and paradox of CBSE's mother tongue directive
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Amruta Veer and Swati Jain

Misti, 6, has just moved with her family from rural Bengal. She sits silently in her first-grade classroom in Gurugram. The teacher points to the blackboard and asks a question in English. Misti speaks Bengali at home. She understands the question vaguely but doesn’t have the words to answer. She knows the answer in her head, but not in this language. So, she stays quiet.

On May 22, 2025, the Central Board of Secondary Education (CBSE) issued a landmark directive that could reshape how language is used in Indian primary classrooms. It stated that the first language of literacy (R1), the language in which the child first learns to read and write should be the child’s mother tongue. Where this is not practical due to linguistic diversity in the classroom, the most familiar local language may be used instead. Crucially, the directive also emphasized that R1 should serve as the medium of instruction (MOI) for all other subjects, including the teaching of second languages. Though the directive uses the term ‘mother tongue,’ in India, this is rarely a single or easily identifiable language. Most children these days grow up in multilingual households and communities, and what counts as their ‘first language’ may shift across context, caregiver, or dialect.

From a pedagogical standpoint, the directive is well-founded. Converging evidence from neuroscience and language education strongly supports mother tongue instruction in the early years (UNESCO, 2003). One of the most influential voices in this space, Jim Cummins, distinguishes between Basic Interpersonal Communication Skills (BICS) and Cognitive Academic Language Proficiency (CALP). BICS refers to everyday conversational fluency, whereas CALP is the proficiency required to understand and use the abstract, formal language needed for academic learning. He argues that children may appear fluent in a second language, but that doesn’t mean they can grasp abstract academic concepts in it. When children first develop these concepts in their mother tongue, they can later transfer them to other languages more easily.

The Sapir-Whorf Hypothesis on linguistic relativity suggests that language does not merely reflect thought; it shapes it. In this light, a child's language is far more than a tool for communication; it is a cognitive framework that shapes their understanding and experience of reality. When this language is excluded from the classroom, what is lost is not just a mode of expression, but an entire way of knowing. Teaching in a language unfamiliar to the child places undue cognitive burden on young learners, often resulting in poor comprehension, disengagement, and long-term learning gaps. Educators are aware of this. Sounds, rhymes, and words are infused with emotional and cultural significance. Teaching that draws on these elements nurtures confidence and self-expression. Teaching that disregards them risks silencing voices and erasing identities.

Yet, ground realities pull in opposite direction. In India, English is seen as the language of aspiration and mobility. This belief fuels the growth of low-fee English-medium schools – especially in urban areas – even when teachers lack the proficiency to deliver real instruction in English. Parents, often non-English speakers themselves, see these schools as tickets to better futures. As a result, children often sit in classrooms they do not understand, taught by teachers who struggle with the same language.

The policy dilemma is real. Early English instruction may harm foundational learning. But delaying English exposure may limit future opportunities. As educationist Krishna Kumar has pointed out, our system is used to treating language as a subject, not as a medium of intellectual development.

India has attempted multilingual education before. The Three-Language Formula (1968) aimed to balance regional identity, national unity, and global aspiration. The National Curriculum Framework (2005) took a more progressive approach, encouraging the use of children’s home languages as classroom resources. The National Focus Group on the Teaching of Indian Languages (2006) argues that instruction in the mother tongue is not just good pedagogy, but it is vital to national reconstruction. It positions language as a vehicle not just for communication, but for cultural transmission, civic participation, identity-building, and breaking the monopoly of knowledge held by the elite. In a society as stratified as India, the classroom can become a place where this linguistic empowerment begins. NCFSE 2023 recommends that the medium of instruction in schools should be the language most familiar to children in a given region. The CBSE directive offers a fresh opportunity, but only if it is followed by real action.

Implementation will not be easy. India’s linguistic landscape is vast and varied. Children often grow up speaking multiple languages and dialects. As scholar Ajit Mohanty notes, children’s linguistic repertoires are complex, and assigning a single "mother tongue" can be arbitrary. In cities like Delhi, classrooms bring together children with no common mother tongue. Rural classrooms may seem more uniform, but children may speak tribal or non-standard dialects that are not recognized in school. Social hierarchies get reflected in the language hierarchies. Dominant caste languages are more likely to appear in textbooks, while Adivasi and Dalit dialects are left out. When language is sidelined in education policy, it is the marginalized children who fall silent first. Assuming ‘one standard regional language fits all’ ignores this reality.

The CBSE directive gestures toward flexibility: allowing the use of the next most familiar language, and encouraging phased transitions. But meaningful implementation demands preparation:

  • Thoroughly understanding the linguistic landscape of different regions across the country.
  • Sensitizing curriculum developers to the value of less powerful local language varieties.
  • Teacher training in bilingual teaching methods, along with support to manage high workloads and language fluency gaps.
  • Developing textbooks and supporting educational materials in diverse languages.
  • Parent communication to build trust in mother tongue-based instruction and clarify that English will still be taught.

There is also a deeper misalignment between policy and assessment. Even if early instruction is in the mother tongue, most state and national exams continue to be in English or Hindi. As Krishna Kumar has argued, the system tends to assess children not on what they know, but on whether they can express it in English. Until we align the language of teaching with the language of testing, this inequity will persist. Technology adds another layer. Most EdTech platforms are built in English or standard Hindi, excluding millions of children learning in regional or tribal languages. Without inclusive language design, NEP 2020's promise of equitable digital education will remain unfulfilled.

We need to examine the construct of a mother tongue and what it means for the child growing up in an inherently multilingual environment. Rather than choosing between languages, welcoming and honouring the multilingualism of the classroom offers a practical way forward. Instead of forcing a binary choice, India needs a sequenced, multilingual approach, where the mother tongue forms the foundation and English is introduced gradually and meaningfully. It allows students to use their full linguistic repertoire fluidly, moving between languages as needed to support comprehension and expression.

It is also crucial to separate English-medium instruction from English language learning. English can be taught effectively without being the medium of instruction, especially in early grades. As Maxine Berntsen, a linguist and longtime advocate for mother tongue-based education in India, explains, oral exposure, interactive language environments, and specifically trained teachers are far more effective than using English as the default MOI from day one.

Veteran educationist bell hooks reminds us that students should be active participants in their learning, not passive recipients of textbook knowledge. Education, she argues, is about developing critical awareness and engaging fully with the world. When classrooms value students’ own languages, learning becomes more meaningful. This creates space for deeper understanding and for the child’s language to become a bridge, not a barrier, to learning English. Ultimately, education is not just about literacy or jobs. It is about identity, imagination, and voice. If mother tongue instruction is to mean anything, teachers need time, training, and freedom to use methods like storytelling, drama, and art, which flourish best in languages that children understand and love.

The legendary Kenyan writer, Ngũgĩ wa Thiong’o, once wrote that the most powerful weapon of imperialism was the cultural bomb: the destruction of a people’s belief in their own names, languages, and environment. In his own career, Ngũgĩ famously moved from writing in English to writing in Kikuyu, challenging the use of a coloniser’s language to capture indigenous realities. His work highlights the paradox of relying on English as a language of expression in classrooms teeming with cultural plurality and vibrant multilingualism.

The CBSE directive challenges us to ask: What happens when a child cannot dream in the language she loves? Can an education system rooted in borrowed tongues ever be truly emancipatory?

The CBSE has opened a door. It remains to be seen whether our education system will walk through it, not only to affirm the right of each child to learn in their language, but to dream in it as well.

References

  • Cummins, J. (1979/1984). Cognitive/academic language proficiency, linguistic interdependence, the optimum age question, and some other matters. Working Papers on Bilingualism, 19, 121-129.
  • Hooks, B. (2014). Teaching to transgress. Chapter 1. Engaged Pedagogy. Routledge.
  • Lucy, J. A. (2016). Linguistic relativity. In E. N. Zalta (Ed.), The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy (Summer 2016 Edition).
  • Mohanty, A. K. (2009). Multilingual education for social justice: Globalising the local. In T. Skutnabb-Kangas, R. Phillipson, A. K. Mohanty, & M. Panda (Eds.), Multilingual education for social justice: Globalising the local (pp. 1-20). Orient BlackSwan.
  • National Council of Educational Research and Training. (2023). National Curriculum Framework for School Education. https://ncert.nic.in/pdf/NCFSE-2023-August_2023.pdf
  • Ngũgĩ wa Thiong’o. (1986). Decolonising the mind: The politics of language in African literature. James Currey.
  • UNESCO. (2003). Education in a multilingual world. UNESCO. https://unesdoc.unesco.org/ark:/48223/pf0000129728

Amruta Veer is a special educator and is associated with a Mumbai-based NGO. She can be reached at amrutasveer@gmail.com.

Swati Jain is a PhD researcher in Education at Tata Institute of Social Sciences, Mumbai. She can be reached at gswatijain@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Amruta Veer and Swati Jain
Language is not just a tool we use to communicate – it is the very framework through which we think, learn, and make sense of the world. This article refers to CBSE’s 2025 directive on mother tongue instruction and highlights how excluding a child’s home language from the classroom can disrupt not just expression, but understanding itself. Why do we continue to equate English with aspiration even as research points for mother tongue instruction?

item.title: Indian citizenship and universal suffrage
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Prakash Iyer

Ira: Each time we talk about the Constitution and thedebatesthey had on every single issue, I am fascinated by the intelligence, foresight, patience, and humility that the Constituent Assembly maintained throughout this process. They had to imagine themselves as regular Indian citizens and not have a patronizing air towards the larger population. Especially, since they were all erudite academics.

Anubhuti: The complexities of the arguments they had could easily have led to big fights, but they maintained protocol, debated calmly, and worked towards a consensus. Remember how a few members of the Assembly considered the tribal populations as backward and how Nichols-Roy pointed out that some practices of the tribal populations were more democratic and far less feudal than the rest of India? No wonder they were able to decide on universal suffrage – voting rights for everyone above the age of 21 years.[i]

Ira: Oh yes. That was a very important decision. Even England had given voting rights to women only as late as 1928. It happened gradually in multiple phases. Didn’t our Constituent Assembly want to consider a gradual move to universal suffrage, given that a large percentage of our population was uneducated? Democratic processes need to be learnt.

Anubhuti: Well, we had already started one step at a time with the British. The Montague-Chelmsford Reforms awarded voting rights to the educated and to property owners who paid tax and allowed provinces to decide if women would have voting rights. We also had electorates reserved for some communities based on religion and depressed classes. Later, the Government of India Act 1935 included women, people of the depressed classes, and people of some minority religions. The same criteria of education, property, and age applied to these groups also. The Constituent Assembly decided to make it universal suffrage.

But Thakur Das Bhargava had raised significant issues related to the complexity of elections across the nation. The constituencies would be larger than they were until then, and the population would increase from 40,000 to over 9 lakh voters per constituency. His concern was that the elections would not be real since people would not even know the representatives they were voting for. He suggested that we focus on education and increase voting rights gradually over the years, otherwise the elections would become a farce.

Ira: This seems quite valid, given that technology wasn’t good enough to campaign and familiarize voters with the representatives. Today, so much of this happens on social media and the internet.

Anubhuti: Alladi Krishnaswami Ayyar’s response to this was very apt and it formed the foundations for universal suffrage. He argued for the basic principle of democracy and said, “If democracy is to be broad based and the system of governments that is to function is to have the ultimate sanction of the people as a whole, in a country where the large mass of the people are illiterate and the people owning property are so few, the introduction of any property or educational qualifications for the exercise of the franchise would be a negation of the principles of democracy. If any such qualifications were introduced, that would have disfranchised a large number of the labouring classes and a large number of women-folk. It cannot after all be assumed that a person with a poor elementary education and with a knowledge of the three Rs. is in a better position to exercise the franchise than a labourer, a cultivator or a tenant who may be expected to know what his interests are and to choose his representatives. Possibly a large scale universal suffrage may also have the effect of rooting out corruption what may turn out incidental to democratic election.”[ii] He shared that the subcommittee for Fundamental Rights had thought through the basic principles of democracy and ensured that the educated few did not end up patronizing the others and furthering epistocracy[iii] rather than democracy.

Ira: I completely agree. Mere literacy or school education does not make us more perceptive and careful about choosing our representatives. It does not increase our investment in the democratic process any more than others who may not be literate. What is the corruption he is referring to at the end?

Anubhuti: He seems to be referring to the corruption of the representatives and government officials which was rampant even in those times. If elections are open to everyone, they will not be able to think of themselves as representing the landed gentry and rich people alone. They would be answerable to the larger population, at least for the elections.

Many members of the Constituent Assembly were also concerned about the corruption of the election process itself – whether it should be governed centrally by an independent body or be left to each province to manage and oversee. The Fundamental Rights subcommittee debated this at length and decided that there was a need for an independent body to supervise elections. This again brought up the issue of federalism versus unitarianism.

Ira: You mean the setting up of a central election commission was suggested then itself? The first elections were conducted a year or so after the Constitution was ratified, wasn’t it? So, it was an immediate necessity.

Anubhuti: The debate centred around which arm of the State should monitor and oversee the elections – the Executive or the Judiciary? K. Santhanam argued that conducting elections is such a humungous task that it will require numerous people and state resources. But the incumbent government might be biased and so a small commission ought to be formed with some authority to monitor elections alone, and this should be a judicial commission not an executive commission.[iv]

Ambedkar clarified how the Fundamental Rights sub-committee’s response was a combination of dealing with the federal vs. unitary model and independence of the authority managing elections. “Many people felt that if the elections were conducted under the auspices of the Executive authority and if the Executive authority did have power, as it must have, of transferring officers from one area to another with the object of gaining support for a particular candidate who was a favourite with the party in office or with the Government of the day, that will certainly vitiate the free election which we all wanted. It was therefore unanimously resolved by the members of the Fundamental Rights Committee that the greatest safeguard for purity of election, for fairness in election, was to take away the matter from the hands of the Executive authority and to hand it over to some independent authority. …the Scheme that was in the minds of the members of the Fundamental Rights Committee was that there would be a Central Commission appointed by the President in order to deal with the elections throughout India.”[v]

Ira: Since the President appoints the commission, it kind of goes beyond the Judiciary also. It is answerable to the constitutional authority, the President. The interpretation of the Constitution is the Judiciary’s responsibility, so in a sense they are answerable to the Judiciary too though not directly.

Anubhuti: I like the way you put it. The President is the ultimate constitutional authority, so the commission reporting to the President rather than the Prime Minister or the Chief Justice of the Supreme Court makes sense; in that they are reporting to the Constitution.

Like Alladi Krishnaswami Ayyar pointed out, our Constitution was more progressive than many others, even the US constitution on two counts – special rights given to minorities and depressed classes and single citizenship in a federalism.

We had removed the community specific electorates that the British had created (which is referred to as their Divide and Rule policy) and replaced them with joint electorates thus allowing the possibility of members of Scheduled Caste and Tribes also to gain representation in the electoral process. He also pointed out how we award single citizenship of the Indian state rather than dual citizenship of a central state and provinces, which exists till date in the US.

Ira: You had shared that when we spoke about federalism; that common laws apply to all states, and it is only economic and other areas where individual states have powers to be different from others.

Voting was one of the rights that this subcommittee on Fundamental Rights discussed. How didn’t they think about other rights and duties? That is a complex area, mainly because of the inherent tension between rights and duties.

Anubhuti: Since we are talking about tension between rights and duties, the concept of duties is embedded in every right through limitations on them. Alladi Krishnaswami Ayyar pointed this out as a response to criticism by some members that setting limitations on rights strikes at the foundations of the rights themselves. He said, “…we wanted to speak of not merely fundamental rights but also fundamental duties. But it did not look as if these were capable of being tabulated, because in the first instance every right implies and includes a duty. What is my right is my neighbour’s duty to me. The right of the wife to equality with the husband is the duty of the husband towards the wife in respect of the matter of equality. …These go together. They are opposites, rather they are the obverse and the reverse of the coin, and the criticism that has been levelled by some friends in this House that the duties were not mentioned, is not quite correct because every right implies and includes a duty.”[vi]

Ira: That was so eloquently put. I always wondered about the dichotomy between rights and duties, freedom and equality, unitary and federal. For instance, we think of freedom in individual terms, but by awarding the same freedom to everyone, we are also claiming formal equality.

Anubhuti: True. Our Constitution does exactly that. Some duties are imposed on all citizens – for instance, abolition of untouchability, while many others can be read in the rights themselves. There was a lot of debate on the State being allowed to impose restrictions on Fundamental Rights, particularly the right to freedom of speech and expression, and the right to freedom of religion.

There is much to discuss on rights and duties. Let’s talk about that next time.

[i] The minimum voting age was changed to 18 years as part of the 61st amendment of the Constitution in 1988.

[ii] https://www.constitutionofindia.net/debates/23-nov-1949/#134546

[iii] Epistocracy considers Knowledge as supreme, and recommends that educated or people who know should have power to rule; unlike democracy where the demos (people) should have the power.

[iv] https://www.constitutionofindia.net/debates/29-july-1947/#141728

[v] https://www.constitutionofindia.net/debates/29-july-1947/#141728

[vi] https://www.constitutionofindia.net/debates/25-nov-1949/#130171

Prakash Iyer teaches Philosophy of Education, Moral and Political Philosophy and Curriculum Theory at Azim Premji University, Bengaluru. The author can be reached at prakash.iyer@apu.edu.in.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Prakash Iyer
Ira and Anubhuti discuss the nuances of our universal suffrage principle – who gets to vote, who gets represented, and what democracy should look like in a newly independent nation. They also revisit the arguments presented by Alladi Krishnaswami Ayyar and Ambedkar, who had highlighted why restricting voting rights based on education or possession of property would have undermined democracy itself.

item.title: From theory to reality
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Kalpita Kshirsagar

Should the aim of schools and learning centers be to make children learn theory at the cost of reality?

Schools create systems for themselves and seem to thrive in the utopia of their own creation. The parameters of success are solely based on academics, which do little justice to the multi-faceted intelligence in the child. How will children become aware of their own intellectual needs and strengths if they are not made aware of them? A career doesn’t just fill the pocket, but also builds a life. Adding to the mix is the approach to errors and the silencing of the inner curiosity in order to get the child to toe the line of preset academic standards.

These systems are so far from how the world operates that a child leaving high school, albeit all of 18, is as clueless about the workings of the world as a new born child is. An exception being that a newborn is incredibly absorbent of its surroundings, but the child stepping out into the world has been trained in the skill of ‘learned isolation’ from the world. Until that point, the message from everywhere has been ‘mind your own business’. One has to score well, one has to keep good company, and one has to make the most of any situation. Where is the space for ‘us’, a community, a collaborative world in all of this? Eighteen whole years are spent moulding the child to think only about themselves. Their understanding of the world is thwarted along with their sense of self-worth, which at best is blurry. When a child steps out into the world, do they know their standing?

I turn the question around from the child to us adults who are part of the educational setup. How many times do we engage in the ‘real’ world as a part of our curriculum? Let’s not put the entire expectations of a well-rounded curriculum on the education department. The national curriculum is intended to be a standardized product. But to that we can add our context and reality.

I am talking about what a single teacher, a single school and its community can intentionally do.

We must decide: no theory will be taught without real-life application. Nothing in isolation, all connected pieces. Connected to the life of the child – the real life. If you are teaching geography, begin by knowing your city inside out, study soil samples and pollution levels of the city. Follow your garbage from the house till it is dumped somewhere outside the city. If it is history you teach, attend historical events that happen in the city. Participate in the environment walks, the protests, the elections. If you are teaching science, get children to look at the science we apply daily in our lives – the mixer grinder in the kitchen, the lifts that take us up and down, the bus that brings them to school.

Instead of lamenting that the education system is devoid of reality, let us build our own bridges from theory to reality and make the system better. Because we too are the system, we have always been.

We want to build a generation to have opinions, respect varied viewpoints, and also gracefully agree to disagree.

Let us shift our focus from the colonial educational goals and climb up the Bloom’s ladder toward more fulfilling and creative educational models.

Can we change the exam system? Not radically and not in the immediate future. But what we can choose is not to be limited by it. I know from experience that the young are ready and capable of change. They are actually yearning for it. To learn for more than just exams and to put their hearts and minds into the learning they find value in. On one occasion, a group of grade 10 girls in my class were quick to point out that our history books do not discuss the role of women in the world wars. We soon began a journey of exploration together. Children are community-driven, they want to usher a change and see its impact. Their entire lives are anchored by it. They find their self-worth here. That group of girls still completed the syllabus on time, and went through the motions of preparing for the exams like everyone else, but what they still remember is the “extra learning” we did. This was just one class, one topic, and one time, but as an educator, I felt something inside me change. I felt the need for a shift.

For this shift to take place, schools have to change. They have to see their power in creating a reality-based learning ecosystem. They can begin this shift with their lessons, internal exams, tests, and experiences over which they have full autonomy. The school parent body must be empowered to support this shift and its intended impact. Parents also see the gap but often feel helpless in the storm of competitions and peer pressure. But once they see their children making a mark, their reservations will also ease.

The idea is for schools to act as bridges between the child and the world rather than be a wall between the two. There is richness in childhood, which the world desperately needs and there are opportunities in the world that children can benefit from. Let us ensure that every child that steps out of school will know ways in which they can enrich the world and make it better, one learner at a time.

Kalpita Kshirsagar is an educator and currently the Principal at The Montessori Academy, Pune. She is keen on curating philosophies and practices that help establish child-centered learning ecosystems and she continues to be a learner of reflective teaching practices. She can be reached at kalpitakshirsagar@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Kalpita Kshirsagar
School was meant to prepare children for the world but learning tends to be confined to textbooks and exams. Amidst this disconnect between the theoretical learning at school and the ever-changing realities of the world, this article shifts the focus to students. What kind of individuals are we shaping in such a system? What would it take for us to ensure today’s students turn into young people who are eager to engage with the world, ask important questions, and make an impact? By connecting learning to real-world contexts, this article shows how educators can help make learning more meaningful.
